source_h2_sections: Why Not One SDLC for All?
learning_objectives: Understand why different projects need different development approaches
Know the three most commonly used software development models
Understand how the Waterfall model works and when to use it
Understand how Agile works and its popular frameworks
Understand the V-Model and when to use it

heading_tree.text: Why Not One SDLC for All?
Project Objectives Every Team Must Balance
Behind the Scenes — Two Key Teams

output.division_reasoning: Block 1 merges 'Why Not One SDLC for All?' and 'What are Software Development Models?' because these two H2 sections are tightly coupled — the first builds the problem context, the second defines the solution. Combined word count is ~280 words, well within range. They form one complete introductory idea. Block 2 is the Waterfall Model — a standalone complete concept with its own how-it-works, when-to-use, advantages, and disadvantages (~210 words). Block 3 is the core Agile Model (concept + workflow + delay problem + when-to-use + advantages + disadvantages, ~320 words) — split here because the Agile section has 9 H3 sub-sections totalling over 600 words when Scrum and Kanban are included. Block 4 is Popular Agile Frameworks (Scrum + Kanban, ~280 words) — split from Agile core because framework details are a separate learnable unit. Block 5 is the V-Model — standalone complete concept (~160 words).

blocks.title: Why Software Development Models Exist
h2_sections_included: Why Not One SDLC for All?
learning_objectives_hint: Understand why a single SDLC approach does not fit all projects
Define what software development models are
Identify the three most commonly used models

learning_objectives_hint: Understand how the Waterfall model works step by step

List the advantages and disadvantages of Waterfall

blocks.title: Understanding the Agile Model
h2_sections_included: Agile Model — core concept through Disadvantages
learning_objectives_hint: Understand how the Agile model works through sprints
Identify when to use Agile
Understand the Dev and Ops team structure in Agile

blocks.title: Popular Agile Frameworks — Scrum and Kanban
learning_objectives_hint: Understand what Scrum is and its key roles
Understand how Kanban boards work
Distinguish between Scrum and Kanban as Agile frameworks

blocks.title: Understanding the V-Model
learning_objectives_hint: Understand what the V-Model is and how it extends Waterfall
Distinguish between Verification and Validation
Identify when to use the V-Model

examples.notes: Near-perfect example. The key decisions: (1) merging the two intro H2s because they are conceptually inseparable at ~280 words combined, (2) splitting Agile from its frameworks because the full Agile section would exceed 600 words with 9 H3s, (3) keeping all V-Model H3s together because the section is small. Block titles are clear and learner-facing — not raw heading text.

learning_objectives: Understand what a prompt is and why prompt engineering matters
Apply prompt templates to real scenarios

output.division_reasoning: Block 1 is 'Prompt Engineering Fundamentals' alone — it is a standalone intro section with its own examples (~180 words) and forms a complete entry point. Block 2 merges '1. Prompt Engineering' + 'Why Prompt Engineering?' + 'Be Clear and Direct' — all three answer the question 'what is prompt engineering and why does it matter'. Combined ~280 words, within range. Note: the title '1. Prompt Engineering' is cryptic so the block is given a descriptive learner-facing title. Block 3 is the full RCATF Framework section — all five component H3s (Role, Context, Action/Task, Format, Tone) must stay together because each component only makes sense as part of the complete framework (~420 words). Block 4 merges 'Example Prompt Templates' + 'Tricks and Tips' + 'Separating Data from Instructions' + 'Additional Exploration' — these are all practical application sections that together complete the session (~280 words combined).

blocks.title: What is a Prompt and Why Does Clarity Matter
learning_objectives_hint: Define what a prompt is in the context of LLMs
Understand why better prompts produce better results

blocks.title: What is Prompt Engineering and Why Does It Matter
learning_objectives_hint: Define prompt engineering
Explain why prompt engineering produces better and more consistent results
Apply the principle of clear and direct instructions

blocks.title: The RCATF Framework — Structuring Your Prompts
learning_objectives_hint: List and explain all five components of the RCATF framework
Construct a well-structured prompt using Role, Context, Action, Format, and Tone

blocks.title: Prompt Templates, Tips, and Further Exploration
learning_objectives_hint: Apply prompt templates to real scenarios
Use data-instruction separation to create reusable prompts
Identify resources for further prompt engineering exploration

examples.notes: Excellent example of title rephrasing. '1. Prompt Engineering' and '3. Basic Prompt Structure' are cryptic numbered headings — both are given clear learner-facing block titles. The RCATF framework is correctly kept together as one block despite having 7 H3s, because all five components are sub-elements of a single concept, not independent topics. The final block correctly aggregates four small closing sections that together complete the session.

source_h2_sections: Core Components of an AI Agent
How Agents Work — The ReAct Pattern
learning_objectives: Define what an AI agent is
Identify the three core components of an AI agent
Understand how the ReAct loop works
Know the steps to build a basic agent

heading_tree.text: Core Components of an AI Agent
How Agents Work — The ReAct Pattern

output.division_reasoning: All four H2 sections are kept as separate blocks. 'What is an AI Agent?' (~150 words) is a clear standalone definition block — too conceptually distinct to merge with Components. 'Core Components' (~220 words, 3 H3s) is one tightly bound concept — all three components (AI Model, Tools, Memory) stay together. 'How Agents Work — ReAct Pattern' (~200 words, 3 H3s) is a complete distinct concept — the three loop steps are inseparable sub-parts of one idea. 'Building Your First Agent' (~180 words) is a standalone practical block. No merges because each section covers a clearly distinct concept. No splits because no section exceeds 300 words.

blocks.title: What is an AI Agent?
learning_objectives_hint: Define what an AI agent is
Distinguish an AI agent from a standard LLM

blocks.title: How AI Agents Work — The ReAct Loop
h2_sections_included: How Agents Work — The ReAct Pattern
learning_objectives_hint: Describe the three stages of the ReAct loop: Thought, Action, Observation
Explain how an agent iterates until it completes a task

blocks.title: Building Your First AI Agent
learning_objectives_hint: List the steps to build a basic AI agent
Identify the tools needed to get started

examples.notes: Good example showing when NOT to merge even though sections are small. 'What is an AI Agent?' (~150 words) could technically be merged with 'Core Components' but the two are conceptually distinct enough to warrant separate blocks — definition vs components. The division reasoning clearly justifies why no merges were made. Note: 'What is an AI Agent?' is acceptable as a block title because the original H2 heading is already learner-facing and clear.
